text: You're right to call out AAA devs pretending to be indies.  It's a totally different world with a completely different work model.  When I was running a small engineering company with my dad, we occasionally hired guys from the big corporate firms.  They lasted so little time.  They were so used to the big, clunky, inefficient, hyper-managed corporate structure that they had no flexibility or ability to think and adapt on their own. replies.author: @PhelanWolfhound
replies.text:
​​@cjvs1604And he made narrative mistakes, too.  Remember, while he was a savant when it came to fight choreography, he was the literary rookie in the room.  The rest of the RWBY writing team have more experience with writing more traditional stories in this medium, have shown that experience on occasion in RWBY, but they knew who their audience was, were at the forefront of using the Internet to have direct communication with said audience (which made it easier for them to engineer their expectations with the info they released), and dumbed it down to a mere flash-and-spetacle show.  It's why you saw so many narrative corners being cut, even early on: they knew the vast majority of their audience wouldn't notice, or would blindly support the brand even if they did.

I would agree that early RWBY (the first two seasons, season 3 is really where the rot actually starts) is a more effectively written narrative than later stuff, but with the benefit of hindsight, even the early stuff isn't that good.

author: @gaiusjuliuscaesar9902
text:
No, no. The notion that "gamers hate LGBT stories" isn't inherently wrong, just worded a bit too severely. LGBT stories are, for the most part, cringe, pandering, and wholly unrelatable. Even when well written, the concept isn't something 95% of the population can relate with. Most people are straight, so yes, romances featuring straight people tend to be more appealing to them. The LGBT communities themselves acknowledge this phenomena when stating that "they need these stories, so they can relate to the characters", but they miss, or exclude rather, that straight people would feel similarly. I suppose, in their perspective, it is an issue that we feel the way we do, but they can feel the same way, and not only is it okay, but it's something that needs to be supported and addressed with urgency, and remedial action, to cater to them.

In addition to not recognizing this, they fail, fundamentally, to grasp that if people prefer media centered around relatable characters, and that its hard to relate to a romance that doesn't represent you in any sort of way, the vast majority of people will not be interested lgbt stories, especially as concerns the global stage.

Ultimately, that leaves the only ones buying these types of games to be a small minority of LGBT people, people with fetishes, and activists.

But yes, 1348 is also just genuinely a horrible game, made by horrible people.
